analogy: Think of "Forest and Wildlife Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: We share this planet with millions of other living beings, starting from micro-organisms and bacteria, lichens to banyan trees, elephants and blue whales. This entire habitat that we live in has immense biodiversity. We humans along with all living organisms form a complex web of... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Forest and Wildlife Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that we share this planet with millions of other living beings, starting from micro-organisms and bacteria, lichens to banyan trees, elephants and blue whales.

The text explains that this entire habitat that we live in has immense biodiversity.

The text explains that we humans along with all living organisms form a complex web of ecological system in which we are only a part and very much dependent on this system for our own existence.

The text explains that for example, the plants, animals and micro-organisms re-create the quality of the air we breathe, the water we drink and the soil that produces our food without which we cannot survive.

The text explains that forests play a key role in the ecological system as these are also the primary producers on which all other living beings depend.

The text explains that flora and Fauna in India If you look around, you will be able to find that there are some animals and plants which are unique in your area.

The text explains that in fact, India is one of the world’s richest countries in terms of its vast array of biological diversity.

The text explains that this is possibly twice or thrice the number yet to be discovered.

The text explains that you have already studied in detail about the extent and variety of forest and wildlife resources in India.

The text explains that you may have realised the importance of these resources in our daily life.

The text explains that these diverse flora and fauna are so well integrated in our daily life that we take these for granted.

The text explains that but, lately, they are under great stress mainy due to insensitivity to our environment.

The text explains that my Lord, you are the creator of music in the world of Lepchas Oh Narak!

The text explains that my Lord, let me dedicate myself to you Let me gather your music from the springs, the rivers, the mountains, the forests, the insects and the animals Let me gather your music from the sweet breeze and offer it to you Lepcha folk song Biodiversity or Biological Diversity is immensely rich in wildlife and cultivated species, diverse in form and function but closely integrated in a system through multiple network of interdependencies.

The text explains that find out stories prevalent in your region which are about the harmonious relationship between human beings and nature.

The text explains that conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India Conservation in the background of rapid decline in wildlife population and forestry has become essential.

The text explains that 15 FOREST AND WILDLIFE RESOURCES much of its forest and wildlife resources are either owned or managed by the government through the Forest Department or other government departments.

The text explains that reserved forests are regarded as the most valuable as far as the conservation of forest and wildlife resources are concerned.

The text explains that types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources Even if we want to conserve our vast forest and wildlife resources, it is rather difficult to manage, control and regulate them.

The text explains that 17 FOREST AND WILDLIFE RESOURCES Sacred groves - a wealth of diverse and rare species Nature worship is an age old tribal belief based on the premise that all creations of nature have to be protected.

The text explains that the clear lesson from the dynamics of both environmental destruction and reconstruction in India is that local communities everywhere have to be involved in some kind of natural resource management.

The text explains that multiple choice questions (i) Which of the following conservation strategies do not directly involve community participation?

The text explains that protected forests Forests are regarded as most valuable as far as the conservation of forest and wildlife resources.

The text explains that 18 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES 1.

The text explains that (a) Joint forest management (c) Chipko Movement (b) Beej Bachao Andolan (d) Demarcation of Wildlife sanctuaries 2.

The text explains that reserved forests Other forests and wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals and communities.

The text explains that unclassed forests Forest lands are protected from any further depletion.

The text explains that why is biodiversity important for human lives?

The text explains that (ii) How have human activities affected the depletion of flora and fauna?

The text explains that (i) Describe how communities have conserved and protected forests and wildlife in India?

The text explains that (ii) Write a note on good practices towards conserving forest and wildlife.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II, Chapter 2: Forest and Wildlife Resources

prelims_traps: Do not treat 14 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II and 15 FOREST AND WILDLIFE RESOURCES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Forest and Wildlife Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Forest and Wildlife Resources through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 14 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II and 15 FOREST AND WILDLIFE RESOURCES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Forest and Wildlife Resources, which statement is supported by the source text about 18 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II?
options: But why do we need to conserve our forests and wildlife?
Conservation preserves the ecological diversity and our life support systems – water, air and soil.
It also preserves the genetic diversity of plants and animals for better growth of species and breeding.
For example, in agriculture, we are still dependent on traditional crop varieties.
